Debt, Cash Flow, and OpenAI: The 3 Focus Areas for Investors in Q2

To say ORCL’s shares have been on a roller coaster ride since its Q1 report would be an understatement. Post the Q1 report, shares surged and subsequently reached all-time highs, thanks to a 359% y/y surge in the company’s remaining performance obligations (RPOs) as well as a whopping 1,529% y/y in multi-cloud database revenues. The company, around this time, also sealed a deal with ChatGPT parent OpenAI to sell $300 billion in computing power over roughly five years.

However, since reaching all-time highs of $345.72, the company’s shares have fallen off a cliff after investors questioned whether the company has the necessary capital to actually fulfil the orders. It also didn’t help that the company, which has negative free cash flows due to its surging capex spending, tapped the bond markets and sold $18 billion in investment-grade bonds in September. There were also reports that the company would be forced to raise “as much as $100 billion” in debt to fund its data centre buildouts.

Moreover, worries surrounding the company’s ability to fulfil its debt obligations was also evidenced last month when there was a surge in buying of credit default swaps tied to ORCL. The CDS five-year spreads tied to ORCL reached as high as 105 bps (or 1.05%) last month, compared to around 38 bps for (GOOGL) and (AMZN), and around 34 bps for (MSFT).

When one factors in all these developments in the last few months, the main point that investors need to look for in the company’s Q2 report is clarity surrounding the company’s funding plans, especially with respect to debt obligations. More specifically, they need to focus on any colour related to the final debt figure that the company plans to raise to build out their AI infrastructure, as well as any colour on when they would expect to be cash flow positive again. Both these factors are critical to estimate whether the company has the financial muscle to fulfil the surging demand. Any abstract deadline or estimates from the management, with no clear rationale, would not be positive, in my view, for the stock.

In addition, investors should also watch out for the company’s guidance on margins. The company, during its analyst day in October, laid out some ambitious goals including gross margins ranging between 30% and 40%, annual EPS of $21 and revenues of $225 billion by FY30. While these are well above analyst estimates and are impressive, investors should look out for the margins that the company is generating today and whether there is any decelerating trend.

Finally, management commentary on any updates with respect to their $300 billion deal with OpenAI would also be a very crucial factor. OpenAI itself requires significant funding to fulfil all its obligations and has been inking deals with all the major AI players. Given that the OpenAI deal is a significant one for ORCL and accounts for a large portion of its impressive RPO, investors, in my view, need to watch out for any progress on this front.

Valuation

ORCL, according to Seeking Alpha, currently trades at a forward P/E of 29.5x, significantly below the 35.5x it was trading during the time I wrote my last article on the company. However, it is still trading well above its 5-year average of 21.3x.

Post the Q1 report, I deployed an Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average (ARIMA) model to forecast ORCL’s FY26 EPS. The model projected an FY26 EPS of $6.16.

The company currently trades at a forward PEG of 1.3, significantly below the sector median of 1.70 and below the company’s own 5-year average of 1.95. The table below shows the projected FY27 EPS based on these different PEG ratios. The forward P/E multiple remains at 29.5x, and the FY26 EPS remains at $6.16 for all three scenarios.

The average projected FY27 EPS comes out to be $7.29. At a forward P/E of 29.5x, the price target for ORCL comes out to be $215, which suggests limited upside from current levels. As such, from a fundamental perspective, I am maintaining my HOLD rating on the stock.

Having said that, as mentioned earlier, ORCL stock has a stellar track record of performing strongly around the earnings release. Based on this track record, heading into the earnings release, investors could take a small position for the purpose of benefiting from a potential post-earnings momentum. Prior to making this move however, investors should keep in mind that such a trade has two risks: (1) The overall AI trade has shown signs of weakness; and (2) ORCL has been under more scrutiny than ever before, especially from a cash flow and debt perspective.

Concluding Thoughts

ORCL has a mixed track record when it comes to beating analyst estimates. However, the market, especially in recent quarters, has given more priority to the company’s RPO as well as the OCI revenue growth. As such, should the company once again deliver strong growth figures on these metrics, I would expect the stock to rally, especially given that the stock is heading into the earnings report beaten down.

For the Q2 print itself, I would look at three key factors: management’s commentary on the company’s plans with respect to raising debt to fund the AI infrastructure buildout, the timeline of when the company plans to be cash flow positive again, and whether any progress has been made with respect to its deal with OpenAI.

From a valuation perspective, I am maintaining my HOLD rating on the stock give the limited upside at current levels. While investors can still make a trade based on the stock’s strong momentum around earnings releases, the risks of increased scrutiny on the company and the relative weakness of the overall AI trade need to be factored in.

data.title: Navios Maritime Partners: Steady As She Goes, Rising Its Fair Value And Still Undervalued
data.description:
I’ve covered Navios Maritime Partners (NMM) before. In my initial article I outlined why I considered it a strong investment and a low risk opportunity; since then, the stock has returned more than 50%. While the adjusted Q3 results were slightly lower than my estimate, the company continues to be an interesting buy and Q4 figures will be even better than last quarter due to better drybulk and tanker rates.

Background

Navios Maritime Partners is the second largest diversified shipping company with a fleet of over 140 on the water vessels and 25 newbuilds across three segments: drybulk, containers and tankers.

The number of vessels on the water has been trending down as the company is selling older vessels faster than newbuilds are delivered:

Navios has plenty of selling candidates with over 50 vessels older than 15 years, I expect the company to continue selling older vessels and benefit from elevated secondhand prices. At the same time, divesting older vessels will reduce the average fleet age that now stands at 9.7 years.

While the company has more drybulk vessels than in the other segments, the three categories carry similar weight, each with a valuation of around $2 billion. However, all containers are fixed on long term contracts, and most tanker vessels are either chartered or have index linked rates, while bulkers are mostly exposed to the spot market.

This is very important as drybulk rates are the highest they have been in many years. Q1 Capesize freight forwards already above $20,000 even though Q1 is typically the weakest quarter.

If rates remain elevated during the year, as Cal26 suggests at $24,000, Navios is perfectly positioned to benefit from it.

Moreover, this could directly benefit shareholders with increased buybacks. The company started buybacks in 2024 and since then has maintained a steady pace. However, if profits rise substantially, the company may decide to accelerate the buyback program.

Financial Position and Valuation

Navios Maritime Partners has a strong financial position with $381 million in cash and $2.2 billion in debt. While debt will continue to increase as newbuild are delivered, the total debt levels are low with a net loan to value of just 35% due to the elevated vessels value exceeding $6 billion. Moreover, debt has a staggered profile without near term maturities and only $200 million due before 2030.

Another benefit, given the current evolution of interest rates, is that about 60% of this debt has floating interest rates. If rates continue to move down, this is a positive tailwind.

Q4 guidance was also very encouraging. When the company reported Q3 earnings, it had already contracted $294 million of revenue, with 12% of days still open, and with the quarter cash operating cost of $208 million. This means that any additional revenue will flow straight to the bottom line. With capsize and tanker rates strengthening over the last few weeks, I assume that the company will easily achieve more than $120 million in operating cash flow. For reference, Q3’s net cash from operating activities was $103 million. This suggests that Q4 EPS should be above $3.5.

Regarding valuation, brokers estimate Navios’ NAV at $137 per share:

The NAV decline from Q4-24 is due to a reduction in tanker values; however, given the recent rates increases, tanker values are soaring again, and NAV should increase in Q4. In fact, NAV has already increased almost 4% since Q2, when it was estimated at $133 per share.

The current stock price implies that the company is trading at 0.38 times NAV, despite improving fundamentals and consistently repurchasing shares. I believe that this valuation is too low, and given the strong underlying rates and outlook, I’m increasing my fair value to $85 per share, about 0.6 times NAV.

Moreover, Darren Maupin has continued to accumulate shares and now owns more than 17% of the company, which is similar to Angeliki Frangou’s holding. This could increase the pressure on Angeliki and improve the company’s valuation.

Risks

The company’s main risk is that it depends entirely on Angeliki Frangou’s (chairwoman of the board and CEO) actions. While she only owns 17% of the units, she controls the GP. However, as most of her wealth is invested in the company, she is the main beneficiary if the stock re-rates higher. On the other hand, she could prioritize her own interest instead of those of the other shareholders.

Another risk is related to rates and asset valuations. Despite much lower rates, the company could continue its current repurchase pace; however, lower rates will reduce ship valuations and NAV will decrease. In that scenario the stock price will probably suffer despite trading at a fraction of NAV.

Conclusion

Navios Maritime Partners continues on its steady path of ordering newbuilds and selling older tonnage while continuing to repurchase shares.

If this strategy is maintained over time, the repurchases add up, and given the huge discount at which the company is trading, it is very accretive. I suspect that sooner or later the market will realize this and close the valuation gap.

Until then, Navios offers a cheap entry to shipping compared to peers with much higher valuations.

data.title: Rare Stock Picks In November 2025 - From 14 Discerning Analysts
data.description:
Although the S&P ended the month about where it started, there was significant volatility in between, including a sharp drop and equally sharp rebound. Given this volatility, are there any compelling investment ideas out there that may have flown under your radar?

Below is a list of November Buy recommendations made by analysts who had no other bullish recommendations over the past 3 months.

For your directory assistance, we've classified the opportunities by sector.

Healthcare

CytomX Therapeutics (CTMX) - ELAM1 Consulting, who focuses on the healthcare sector, says investment in CTMX is high risk and binary, but promising science and early clinical data support a speculative Buy for risk-tolerant investors. - Targeting Adhesion Molecules With ADCs: The Bet Of CytomX

uniQure (QURE) - Longtime analyst Bhavneesh Sharma says shares have dropped more than 50%, already discounting setbacks, AMT-130 is the first disease-modifying therapy for Huntington's, and modeling suggests a 78% probability of eventual approval with near-term catalysts including FDA meeting minutes and EMA discussions. - From Setback To Surge: UniQure's Huntington's Hope Is Poised For Upside

Arrowhead Pharmaceuticals (ARWR) - Longtime analyst John Reilly says it boasts a diversified RNAi pipeline with multiple late-stage candidates and strong efficacy data while it maintains a robust cash runway of 24 months, mitigating near-term financing risks. - Arrowhead Is A Buy On REDEMPLO(TM) Approval And Strong Cash Runway (Editor’s note: Shares have rallied since the article was published)

Eli Lilly (LLY) - Longtime analyst Bill Gunderson says it is poised to become the first $1 trillion pharmaceutical company, driven by explosive growth in its weight-loss drug, Mounjaro, earnings and sales growth have been exceptional, and it is rated a 'Strong Buy' for its rare combination of value and growth. - It Looks Like Eli Lilly Will Become The Next Trillion Dollar Baby

Financials

TFS Financial (TFSL) - Longtime analyst Thomas Lott says it is deeply undervalued, trading at 7x earnings and 39% of book value, with a robust 8.3% dividend yield, and with stable underwriting, conservative management, and potential for EPS growth to $2.60+, TFSL could double if rates fall and loan demand rebounds. - TFS Financial: Huge Bargain At 7x Earnings With An 8%+ Dividend Yield

Rithm Capital (RITM) - Longtime analyst Patrick Harden says it is executing a strategic transformation from a mortgage REIT to an alternative asset manager to unlock higher valuation, while recent acquisitions and new fund launches are rapidly scaling RITM's asset management segment. - Rithm Capital: Why I'm Buying Into Its Transformation Endgame

Blue Owl Capital (OWL) - Longtime analyst Tim Travis says it is a strong buy after recent price declines, offering attractive value amid negative headlines affecting private capital companies; its robust growth is driven by record capital raising, expanding assets under management, and a resilient business model with limited credit risk exposure. - Blue Owl Capital Is A Strong Buy Into The Hyperbolic Headlines

Technology

GitLab (GTLB) - Long/Short investor Banana Stand Capital says it is undervalued amid AI disruption fears, but its security and reliability focus position it to benefit from rising AI-generated code; trading at 6x EV/Sales with 29% revenue growth, GTLB offers attractive upside and is a strong buy, further supported by takeover potential and robust free cash flow margins. - GitLab: AI Is A Catalyst, Not A Threat

Consumer Staples

Armanino Foods of Distinction (OTCPK:AMNF) - Individual investor John Duncan notes that the business is protected by its high-quality food brand and low-cost structure, it prioritizes dividends and buybacks, and it has been compounding revenue, earnings, and cash flow steadily for over a decade. - Armanino Foods of Distinction: A Small-Cap Italian Frozen Food Maker Is Just What I Ordered

Materials

Mosaic (MOS) - Individual investor Nicholas Sherr says it offers an attractive risk/reward profile at current valuations, with 50% upside potential and limited downside, already below tangible book value; tight global phosphate and potash supply, plus strong ag demand, provide downside protection. - Mosaic Has Asymmetric Upside On Critical Infrastructure

Energy

Ecopetrol (EC) - Individual investor Green Mage Capital says it trades as a liquidating trust, effectively assigning zero value to the midstream monopoly or future reserve extensions, and the double-digit yield is funded by organic free cash flow, not debt, paying investors handsomely to wait. - Ecopetrol: Priced Like A Dying Asset, Paying Like A King

CMB.TECH (CMBT) - Longtime analyst J Mintzmyer says that despite macro headwinds and negative sentiment, shipping stocks are set up for strong returns into 2026 with relative valuations at multi-year lows and key bullish drivers; his top pick for 2026 is CMB.TECH, which has a probable 50-100% upside over the next 6-12 months. - Buy Cmb.Tech: Shipping Poised To Outperform Into 2026

CMB.TECH (CMBT) - Longtime analyst Alberto Ayuso Martín says it trades at a significant discount to NAV, offering an attractive entry point for investors, and it benefits from a young, diversified fleet, high time-charter coverage, and exposure to strong dry bulk and tanker markets. - Cmb.Tech: Still Some Juice Left

Real Estate

Rexford Industrial Realty (REXR) - Longtime analyst Colorado Wealth Management Fund notes the increased guidance and growth expectations; management executed $150M in accretive share buybacks and maintains a conservative balance sheet, and leasing activity hit a record high, underlining continued tenant demand and portfolio strength. - Record-Breaking '3.3 Million' Quarter By Rexford

Inclusion Criteria: The above ideas were objectively compiled based on the set criteria. Exclusions include new Seeking Alpha analysts, broad market calls, crypto ideas, leveraged ETFs, and stocks that were repeat recommendations by the analyst.

Thanks for reading.

This account (Rare Stock Picks) will publish future iterations of this report in the upcoming months. If you missed our Rare Stock Picks from October, you can find it here.

data.title: AMD: An AI Giant In The Making
data.description:
I have been bullish on Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD) for a good part of this year and it has worked out very well even after the recent pullback. In early September, I reiterated my buy rating and since then the stock has rallied another ~35% and so I believe it would be an understatement to say that the bulls are pleased. The company reported earnings back in November and today, we'll see if it's time to double down or take some profits.

In the below analysis, it is shown that Q3 was generally a good quarter for AMD with Data Center revenues accelerating. While there was some profitability weakness and Q4 guidance is a bit mixed, the long term outlook offered at their Financial Analyst Day shows that the company is an AI giant in the making. Therefore, I'm fazed by the elevated valuation and in fact view the recent pullback as a prime buying opportunity.

Revenues Show Robust Demand

For 2025 Q3, AMD reported record revenues of $9.2 billion, representing a growth rate of 35.59%. They stated that this strength was driven by strong demand in their Data Center AI, Server, and PC businesses and so key parts of the company are performing well. As you can see in the chart above, growth accelerated from the previous quarter and is basically at the highest level in the past few years. Therefore, I would say that this indicates worries about a slowdown are misplaced at this point. In addition, AMD was able to breeze past expectations by a margin of nearly $500 million and so overall it was a comfortable quarter for the company. Let's now turn to a closer look at their all-important Data Center segment.

Data Center Growth Accelerates

AMD's Data Center business is its largest by revenue and also the most important for the company's strategic positioning. However, it actually isn't the company's largest growth driver currently. For Q3, they reported $4.3 billion in revenues representing growth of 22% as shown above. Note that this is well below the company-wide growth rate discussed above. On the other hand, their Client and Gaming segment saw 73% growth to $4 billion in revenue. Ryzen processors saw record sales and Gaming revenue was up 181% YoY due to robust demand for Radeon GPUs. This is really quite impressive given the consumer sentiment right now but in the long run, there is no question that their Data Center business is the core focus for AMD.

While the 22% growth rate may not seem overly impressive, it actually represents a significant acceleration from Q2's 14% increase and so momentum is strong for this key business. It is already stated in the infographic that they saw strong demand for their Instinct MI350 Series GPUs but AMD was able to secure a strategic partnership with OpenAI that will initially see them rollout 1GW of compute in the form of Instinct MI450 GPUs in the second half of next year. The partnership is for 6GW in total and so for investors that are concerned about Nvidia (NVDA) and Broadcom (AVGO) eating their lunch, the market seems large enough for multiple companies to be successful. The demand for compute really does seem to be insatiable right now. Lastly, you can see that operating margin dipped significantly from the prior year period with operating expenses weighing. We'll discus their company-wide efficiency and profitability below but this could be a potential concern if R&D isn't the driver of this increase in expenses.

Profitability Is Decent But Not Great

In terms of profitability, AMD's results were respectable but not too impressive. As shown above, gross margin didn't change YoY and remains at 54% and so cost control and pricing power have been largely stable. This definitely isn't a bad thing as a gross margin in the mid-50s percentage range is quite strong.

However, as you can see, operating expenses as a percentage of revenue increased by 2 percentage points YoY. That led to operating margin contraction of 1 percentage point to 24%. While this operating margin is also still quite strong, contraction is never desirable. As stated above, if R&D spending led to this lower profitability, it wouldn't be an issue for me. However, looking at their income statement, R&D only increased by 31% to $2.139 billion, less than the percentage increase in revenue. Therefore, corporate efficiency could have been even better in my view. That may be forgivable since the company is trying to rapidly scale their AI solutions.

At the end of the day, AMD reported an adjusted EPS of $1.20, up 30% YoY. This growth rate lags the top line growth rate by a notable margin to demonstrate some shakiness in profitability but still they managed to beat EPS expectations by $0.03. Therefore, it isn't a major worry at this point.

Near Term Outlook

AMD provided guidance for Q4 in their earnings release. With revenues being expected to be $9.6 billion at the midpoint, they are implying growth of 25% YoY. That is a notable slowdown from Q3's growth rate but it is unsure where this deceleration will originate from. If its from their Client & Gaming segment, it would be much less of a worry than if it was from their Data Center segment. In their earnings call, the company did state that they expect gaming revenues to be down strong double digits QoQ and so it seems that the weakness will not be from their key Data Center business.

For gross margin, they are expecting 54.5% and that would be a minimal change from the year ago quarter's 54%. Therefore, again pricing power and cost control will remain rather stable.

Lastly, they project operating expenses of $2.8 billion, representing a 32% rise. With that outpacing the projected top line growth rate, profitability is expected to remain under some pressure in the near term. Overall, while their revenue guidance was above consensus expectations, I would say that the outlook for Q4 is a little mixed.

Long Term Outlook

On their Financial Analyst Day, AMD laid out some very ambitious targets and if they are able to deliver, the company is set to become a computing giant. We'll focus on their Data Center business once again for this discussion. Firstly, they reported that Instinct MI350 Series GPUs are already the "fastest ramping product in company history." Moving forward their Instinct MI450 Series GPUs "are expected to deliver rack-scale performance leadership with industry leading memory capacity and scale-out bandwidth beginning in the third quarter of 2026." Furthermore, the release of MI500 is set for 2027 and so the company clearly has a strong pipeline. In addition to just GPUs, the company's next-gen "Venice" CPUs and networking solutions are high growth opportunities in the data center space. Overall, I believe AMD is positioning itself to be a major player in cutting edge AI compute as they seem to be investing heavily in innovation.

On the financial side of things, for the long term AMD is targeting a 35%+ revenue CAGR with operating margin of over 35%. For their Data Center business in particular, they are aiming for a 60% revenue CAGR. Firstly, both the company-wide and segment specific targets are clearly ambitious and secondly, you can see it is true that in the long run, their Data Center segment is expected to lead the way even though growth lagged their Client & Gaming segment in Q3. For more details on their long term targets, I encourage readers to take a look at their Financial Analyst Day press release but I believe this discussion has been enough to show that the long term growth story of the company remains highly intact at this point.

Valuation Is High But Justified

In my previous article, the forward P/E was reported to be 41.35 and so there has been significant expansion since then to the current reading of 54.24. While the valuation is now a bit elevated, I believe there are strong justifications for this.

In today's analysis, the company has been shown to be overall robust. Sure, there are some profitability pressures but Data Center acceleration is the key takeaway. Furthermore, while I found their Q4 guidance to be a bit mixed, the long term outlook they showed at their Financial Analyst Day proves that short-termism is unwise. The long term growth story is highly intact and if things go anywhere near as good as planned, AMD is set to become a major player in the AI compute space.

Therefore, I believe the valuation deserves to be where it is. In fact, I view the recent pullback as an attractive entry point as fears of a slowdown seem to be overblown.

Conclusion

Let's cut to the chase. AMD is currently seen mainly as an alternative to Nvidia or Broadcom in the AI chip space but there are concrete signs that it will join them in becoming a giant down the road. As stated above, Data Center revenues are accelerating and more importantly, management is clearly confident about their long term ambitions. The MI450 and MI500 are set to push the boundaries of GPU performance and their direction for CPUs and networking is also ambitious. With the outlook as bright as ever for the company, I see the valuation as being justified and the recent pullback due to the November tech scare may in fact be a great buying opportunity. Therefore, I'm reiterating my bullish rating on AMD stock.
